servers.name: kubernetes
servers.description: Read-only Kubernetes access (resources, pods, logs, Helm release listing). --read-only means only readOnlyHint=true tools are exposed at the source at all (write/exec tools aren't just blocked, they don't exist in this process); the tools list below just pins the exact live set explicitly so a future package bump can't silently add to it. helm_list is read-only (lists releases/metadata only, no values/manifest rendering). Cerbos blocks reading Secrets. Runs on the 'vicegerent' docker network to reach the API, which widens the host trust boundary to that network (see host/mcp/README 'Security & trust boundary'). configuration_view is deliberately excluded even though the source marks it readOnlyHint=true: unlike every other tool here, it returns the full kubeconfig including client-certificate-data/client-key-data in plaintext, handing the agent (and anything that can read its transcript) a usable cluster credential. configuration_contexts_list covers the 'what clusters/contexts exist' need with just names and server URLs, no key material.

params.name: kubeconfig
params.prompt: Kubeconfig path for the target cluster (blank = auto-generate from the kind 'vicegerent' cluster)

servers.name: gitlab
servers.description: GitLab MCP, scoped to issue read+write and the merge-request lifecycle short of merging or approving (create/update/review/comment) plus label lookups, plus read-only pipeline status/log tools. No generic git file/branch-write tools (create_or_update_file/push_files/create_branch): the bot has direct SSH access to gitlab.hahomelabs.com (charts/agent/templates/networkpolicy.yaml), so routine git operations should go through git itself, not a GitLab-API tool -- the operator uses this for their own GitLab instance and only wants MCP tools for things git itself cannot do (opening/commenting on/reviewing the MR object). approve_merge_request/unapprove_merge_request are excluded (HAH-64) so the bot can never rubber-stamp its own or anyone else's MR. No repo/group/project allowlist -- the operator controls the bot token's own project scope on gitlab.hahomelabs.com directly, unlike GitHub's broader-scoped token. merge_merge_request, create_repository/create_group/fork_repository, create_pipeline/retry_pipeline/cancel_pipeline (the mutating pipeline tools USE_PIPELINE also unlocks -- only the 5 read ones are allowlisted below), all CI/CD variable tools, execute_graphql (arbitrary API access -- would bypass every other exclusion here), wiki/release/tag/milestone/webhook management, and general repo/code browsing are excluded.

servers.name: github
servers.description: GitHub MCP, scoped to PRs only — no issue tools at all (this operator does not use GitHub issues at work), and no generic git file/branch-write tools since the bot has direct SSH access to github.com and should do routine git operations (branch creation, file edits, pushes) with git itself, not an MCP tool. Remaining surface: get_me, the pull-request lifecycle short of merging or leaving any review/comment text (create/update, get PR status/diff/files/commits/checks), and update_pull_request_branch. create_repository, fork_repository, delete_file, merge_pull_request, and general repo/code browsing (get_file_contents, search_code, etc.) are excluded. add_reply_to_pull_request_comment is excluded: the PR-comment-reply tool carries no author/ownership info the shim could use to distinguish 'reply to a human's comment' from 'reply to the bot's own', so the whole comment-reply surface is removed rather than partially enforced. pull_request_review_write and add_comment_to_pending_review are excluded for the same reason, on operator instruction: the operator does not want the bot leaving ANY comment/review text on a PR — not just replies, but inline pending-review comments (add_comment_to_pending_review) and review-verdict submissions carrying free-text bodies (pull_request_review_write's COMMENT and REQUEST_CHANGES events; APPROVE was already Cerbos-denied before this, but a partial enforcement of 'no APPROVE' left COMMENT/REQUEST_CHANGES text fully open, which is exactly the gap being closed here). There is no argument-level signal that distinguishes an acceptable review call from an unacceptable one — the honest fallback, same pattern as add_reply_to_pull_request_comment, is to remove the whole capability rather than partially enforce it. Scoped at the source via GITHUB_TOOLS and again via the vMCP tools allowlist. The Cerbos guardrail (defs/resource_github.yaml) additionally hard-enforces which owner/repo and branch the mapped tools may target, regardless of what the token itself can reach, and forces every create/update_pull_request to draft: true.

servers.name: tavily
servers.description: Tavily web search and content extraction. No write capability exists (search/extract/crawl/map/research against arbitrary public URLs, nothing that touches anything this platform owns), so the tools list below is the full live set pinned explicitly rather than a restriction.

servers.name: firecrawl
servers.description: Firecrawl web scraping, crawling, and extraction. No write capability exists (every tool reads/scrapes/monitors an arbitrary public URL you supply; nothing touches anything this platform owns), so the tools list below is the full live set pinned explicitly rather than a restriction.

servers.name: notion
servers.description: Notion workspace (official remote MCP; OAuth, browser consent on first start), shared broadly for read. The vMCP allowlist exposes 5 read tools (search, fetch, comments, teams, users — users takes an optional user_id, including "self" for the current user, which replaced Notion's old dedicated get-user/get-self tools) plus 3 content-write tools (create-pages, update-page, create-comment); database/move/duplicate mutations excluded. create-pages is folder-pinned by a Cerbos deny rule (defs/resource_notion.yaml): any parent other than the Scratchpad page is denied outright, with a message naming the correct page_id. update-page and create-comment target an EXISTING page by id and are gated by a live mcp-cerbos-shim ancestry lookup (it calls notion-fetch back through vMCP -- Cerbos has no I/O) against a per-machine allowlist of parent folders (Scratchpad always implicit, plus ${notionAllowedParentPageIds} from cluster-vars.yaml, e.g. DEVOPS/DevSecOps team folders but not Finance); update-page additionally has Cerbos deny replace_content/allow_deleting_content on the pages that do pass. A hard separate-integration split (read broad / write narrow) was infeasible — the org blocks creating internal-integration tokens.

servers.name: linear
servers.description: Linear issues and projects (OAuth; browser consent on first start). Linear's official remote MCP server has grown to a 55-tool surface, including 5 destructive delete_* tools (attachments/comments/customers/status-updates) and newer feature categories (attachments, agent skills, releases, milestones, customers, initiatives, status updates). This allowlist keeps the platform's original functional scope instead: issues/comments/projects/labels/statuses/teams/users/docs, read+write, no deletes. Linear merged its old create_issue/update_issue, create_comment, and create_project/update_project pairs into single save_issue/save_comment/save_project tools (an `id` arg picks update vs create) — those are the names below. The Cerbos guardrail (defs/resource_linear.yaml) confines save_issue calls that supply a team to the DEVOPS team; save_comment/save_project target an existing entity by id and carry no team the shim can verify, so they're unmapped. The newer feature categories and destructive deletes are deliberately excluded pending a follow-up decision on whether to expose them.

servers.name: jira
servers.description: Jira (sooperset/mcp-atlassian container), confined to the CHANGE (Change Management) project. Jira-only: only JIRA_* creds are supplied so Confluence tools stay disabled. JIRA_PROJECTS_FILTER=CHANGE scopes search/discovery to that project; jira_get_all_projects and jira_get_project_versions are dropped to remove cross-project enumeration. Read+write (issues, JQL search, transitions, comments) scoped at the source via ENABLED_TOOLS and again via the vMCP tools allowlist; destructive deletes are excluded. The CHANGE boundary is hard-enforced by the Cerbos guardrail (defs/resource_jira.yaml): the mcp-cerbos-shim maps every project/issue-bearing tool and Cerbos denies any call whose project_key — or the project prefix of an issue/epic key — isn't CHANGE; JIRA_PROJECTS_FILTER additionally confines free-form search server-side. jira_batch_get_changelogs is dropped because its list-of-keys arg can't be shim-inspected. NB the official remote (io.github.stacklok/atlassian-remote) does NOT work through our vMCP — it advertises the resources capability but returns method-not-found for resources/list, so the vMCP health probe drops it from aggregation.

servers.name: grafana
servers.description: Grafana observability, read-only. Exposed tools scoped via the vMCP tools allowlist (search/datasource/dashboard/prometheus/asserts/annotations/rendering reads); --disable-write is the fail-closed write kill-switch; --disable-proxied turns off Tempo-datasource MCP discovery (unused here) which otherwise blocks every new session on a live call to the real Grafana instance -- without it, being off-VPN makes ALL sessions across every vMCP backend hang, not just this one; Cerbos additionally blocks reads of OpenSearch datasources.

servers.name: grafana_gov
servers.description: Grafana observability (gov-cloud instance) -- otherwise identical to 'grafana' (see that entry for the tool/write-lockdown and --disable-proxied rationale). Separate secrets (grafana_gov_url/grafana_gov_service_account_token) so it points at its own instance.

servers.name: alertmanager
servers.description: Prometheus Alertmanager (alerts, silences, alert groups, status). Full read+write, including createSilence/deleteSilence — the tools list below is the full live 12-tool set pinned explicitly rather than a restriction.

servers.name: alertmanager_gov
servers.description: Prometheus Alertmanager (gov-cloud instance) -- otherwise identical to 'alertmanager' (see that entry for the tool-set rationale). The url param is secret-backed and auto-namespaced (alertmanager_gov_url), so it can't collide with alertmanager's.

servers.name: pagerduty
servers.description: PagerDuty, trimmed to read-only incident/schedule/on-call/service/team/user lookups plus ack/resolve/note on existing incidents (manage_incidents, add_note_to_incident) via --tools. create_incident, add_responders, and start_incident_workflow are intentionally NOT exposed — the operator only wants triage actions on incidents PagerDuty already created, not incident creation or responder escalation from the agent. manage_incidents is further scoped by Cerbos (defs/resource_pagerduty.yaml) to ack/resolve-only field changes (no bulk-count cap — a field allowlist makes batch size irrelevant to the risk); PagerDuty's own manage_incidents body has no service_id field, so true per-service scoping isn't mechanically available here.

servers.name: pagerduty_gov
servers.description: PagerDuty (gov-cloud instance) -- otherwise identical to 'pagerduty' (see that entry for the tool/scoping rationale). Separate secret (pagerduty_gov_user_api_key) so it authenticates against its own account.
